title: Compare DNS Answers Across Resolvers
hook: One resolver can still have the old edge IP while another has the new one.
problem: A site behaves differently for different users and you need to compare DNS answers from multiple public resolvers.
command: for r in 1.1.1.1 8.8.8.8 9.9.9.9; do printf '%s ' "$r"; dig @"$r" +short edge.test A; done
starting_state: A domain may be cached or propagated differently across recursive resolvers.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command sends read-only DNS queries.
when_to_use: Use during DNS cutovers, CDN moves, or reports that only some users reach the wrong IP.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume public resolver agreement proves every ISP cache has updated.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Resolver IPs followed by the A record each resolver returns.

commands: for r in 1.1.1.1 8.8.8.8 9.9.9.9; do printf '%s ' "$r"; dig @"$r" +short edge.test A; done

shorts.title: Compare DNS resolvers.
shorts.voiceover: If users disagree about where a site points, ask multiple resolvers. One stale answer can explain the split.
shorts.caption: Resolver disagreement is a DNS clue.

linkedin.question: When users hit different edge IPs, which resolvers do you compare first?
linkedin.cta: Compare resolver answers before changing the server.

youtube.description: Compare A record answers across public resolvers.

title: Compare Authoritative Nameserver Answers
hook: The recursive resolver was not the problem. One nameserver disagreed.
problem: A domain has multiple authoritative nameservers and you need to see whether they return the same edge IP.
command: for ns in $(dig +short NS edge.test); do printf '%s ' "$ns"; dig @"$ns" +short edge.test A; done
starting_state: A zone has at least two authoritative nameservers.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists NS records and asks each nameserver for the A record.
when_to_use: Use when propagation looks inconsistent even after recursive caches should have expired.
when_not_to_use: Do not edit zone data from this output alone; confirm which provider owns the live zone.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Each authoritative nameserver followed by its A record answer.

commands: for ns in $(dig +short NS edge.test); do printf '%s ' "$ns"; dig @"$ns" +short edge.test A; done

shorts.title: Ask each nameserver.
shorts.voiceover: When authoritative nameservers disagree, caches keep spreading different answers. Query each source directly.
shorts.caption: Check the DNS source.

linkedin.question: Have you traced a DNS issue back to mismatched authoritative nameservers?

youtube.description: Compare authoritative nameserver A record answers.

title: Show the DNS Answer TTL
hook: The fix was correct. The TTL explained why users still saw the old edge.
problem: You need to see how long a DNS answer can remain cached.
starting_state: A domain has an A record answer with a cache TTL.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints the answer section including TTL.
when_to_use: Use during DNS cutovers or when deciding whether stale answers are expected.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat the displayed TTL as the original zone TTL after a resolver has already cached it.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A DNS answer line showing name, TTL, class, type, and value.

shorts.title: Read the DNS TTL.
shorts.voiceover: The answer can be right and still take time to reach users. The TTL tells you why.
shorts.caption: TTL explains stale DNS windows.

linkedin.question: Do you check TTL before calling a DNS cutover broken?

youtube.description: Show the TTL on a DNS A record answer.

title: Check the WWW CNAME Target
hook: The apex was right. The www name pointed through a different path.
problem: You need to see whether www is an alias and where it ultimately resolves.
starting_state: A www hostname may be a CNAME to the apex or another edge hostname.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command queries DNS records only.
when_to_use: Use when apex and www behave differently after a CDN or hosting change.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume a CNAME target is healthy; resolve and test the target separately.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The CNAME target for the www hostname.

shorts.title: Check the www alias.
shorts.voiceover: If apex works and www fails, inspect the CNAME path before changing TLS or web config.
shorts.caption: www can take a different DNS path.

linkedin.question: Do you test apex and www as separate DNS paths?
linkedin.cta: Check the CNAME before editing redirects.

youtube.description: Check the CNAME target and resolved A record for a www hostname.

title: Compare A and AAAA Records
hook: IPv4 worked. IPv6 sent users to a different edge.
problem: A site works for some users but fails for IPv6-capable clients, so you need to compare IPv4 and IPv6 DNS.
command: printf 'A '; dig +short edge.test A; printf 'AAAA '; dig +short edge.test AAAA
starting_state: A hostname may publish both A and AAAA records.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads DNS and compares address families.
when_to_use: Use when mobile networks, certain ISPs, or IPv6 clients report a different failure.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove IPv6 records without confirming the intended edge and provider behavior.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The IPv4 and IPv6 DNS answers for the same hostname.

commands: printf 'A '; dig +short edge.test A; printf 'AAAA '; dig +short edge.test AAAA

shorts.title: Check IPv4 and IPv6.
shorts.voiceover: A site can be healthy over IPv4 and broken over IPv6. Compare A and AAAA before blaming the app.
shorts.caption: IPv6 can be the broken path.

linkedin.question: How often do you check AAAA records during edge incidents?
linkedin.cta: Compare address families before changing services.

youtube.description: Compare A and AAAA answers and test IPv4 versus IPv6 paths.

title: Check CAA Certificate Issuers
hook: The certificate request failed because DNS allowed the wrong issuer.
problem: You need to see which certificate authorities are allowed to issue for a domain.
starting_state: A domain may publish CAA records that constrain certificate issuance.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command queries DNS CAA records.
when_to_use: Use before debugging failed certificate issuance or changing ACME providers.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume missing CAA is a failure; many domains intentionally omit CAA records.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: CAA issue and contact records for the domain.

shorts.title: Check CAA before certs.
shorts.voiceover: If certificate issuance fails, check CAA. DNS may be telling the CA it is not allowed.
shorts.caption: CAA controls who can issue.

linkedin.question: Do you check CAA records before changing ACME tooling?

youtube.description: Query CAA records to see which certificate authorities are allowed.

title: Show TLS Certificate Dates
hook: The outage was not the web server. The edge certificate had expired.
problem: You need to read the served TLS certificate validity window.
command: openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
starting_state: A TLS endpoint presents a certificate for the requested server name.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command performs a read-only TLS handshake and prints certificate dates.
when_to_use: Use when browsers report certificate expiry or a monitoring check says TLS is invalid.
when_not_to_use: Do not use local certificate files when you need to know what the edge is actually serving.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: notBefore and notAfter dates from the served certificate.

commands: openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates

shorts.title: Read served cert dates.
shorts.voiceover: Check the certificate the edge actually serves, not just the file you think Nginx uses.
shorts.caption: The live certificate is what users see.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect the served certificate or the local cert file first?
linkedin.cta: Read dates from the live TLS handshake.

youtube.description: Use openssl s_client and x509 to show live certificate dates.

title: Show TLS Certificate Names
hook: The cert was valid, but not for this hostname.
problem: You need to inspect the certificate subject and SAN names served for a hostname.
command: openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName
starting_state: A TLS endpoint presents a certificate with subject and SAN metadata.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command performs a read-only TLS handshake and prints certificate identity fields.
when_to_use: Use when browsers report a hostname mismatch or a CDN serves the wrong certificate.
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on the CN alone; modern clients validate SAN names.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

commands: openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject
openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName

shorts.title: Check cert names.
shorts.voiceover: A certificate can be unexpired and still wrong. Check the SAN list for the hostname users typed.
shorts.caption: Validity is not hostname coverage.

linkedin.question: When TLS fails, do you check expiry or SAN coverage first?

youtube.description: Inspect TLS certificate subject and SAN names from the live endpoint.

title: Check the Certificate Served for SNI
hook: The IP was right. The SNI name selected the wrong certificate.
problem: You need to prove which certificate an edge returns for a specific SNI hostname.
command: openssl s_client -connect 203.0.113.10:443 -servername wrong.edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName
starting_state: A shared edge IP may serve different certificates based on SNI.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command performs a read-only TLS handshake with an explicit server name.
when_to_use: Use when the same IP serves multiple sites or a CDN returns the default certificate.
when_not_to_use: Do not omit -servername when testing hostname-specific TLS behavior.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The certificate identity fields selected by the requested SNI name.

commands: openssl s_client -connect 203.0.113.10:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName
openssl s_client -connect 203.0.113.10:443 -servername wrong.edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName

shorts.title: Test TLS with SNI.
shorts.voiceover: On shared edge IPs, the server name chooses the certificate. Test with SNI before blaming DNS.
shorts.caption: SNI decides the cert.

linkedin.question: Have you debugged a TLS issue that was really missing or wrong SNI?

youtube.description: Use explicit SNI to see which certificate an edge returns.

title: Show TLS Protocol and Cipher
hook: The certificate was fine. The TLS negotiation told the rest of the story.
problem: You need to see which TLS protocol and cipher the edge negotiates.
command: openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | awk '/Protocol|Cipher|Verify return code/ {print}'
starting_state: A TLS endpoint accepts handshakes for a hostname.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command performs a read-only TLS handshake and filters the negotiated details.
when_to_use: Use when old clients fail, scanners flag TLS settings, or you need a quick negotiation snapshot.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat one client handshake as a full TLS policy audit.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Protocol, cipher, and verification result from the TLS handshake.

commands: openssl s_client -connect edge.test:443 -servername edge.test </dev/null 2>/dev/null | awk '/Protocol|Cipher|Verify return code/ {print}'

shorts.title: Show TLS negotiation.
shorts.voiceover: When TLS complaints are vague, print the protocol, cipher, and verify result from the handshake.
shorts.caption: Handshake details beat guessing.

linkedin.question: What is your quickest TLS negotiation check during an incident?
linkedin.cta: Print protocol, cipher, and verify result.

youtube.description: Filter openssl s_client output for TLS protocol, cipher, and verification details.
